items.question_text: How should flight level 275 be spoken?

choices.text: Flight level two seven five.
Flight level two seventy-five.
Two seven five flight level.
Flight level two seventy-five hundred.

items.explanation: Flight levels are spoken with the words "flight level" followed by the separate digits.

items.situation: You are issuing the surface wind of 030 at 25 knots. Build the transmission.
items.target_phrase: WIND ZERO THREE ZERO AT TWO FIVE

items.situation: A controller wants to issue frequency 126.55 as "one twenty-six point fifty-five" the first time because it sounds faster.
items.para_context: Under 2-4-17, frequencies are first stated with separate digits and the word point where the decimal occurs. Group-form clarification belongs later only if extra clarity is needed.

choices.text: State it first as "one two six point five five."
State it first as "one twenty-six point fifty-five" because group form is always preferred.
State only "point five five" once the aircraft is already on your frequency.
Drop the decimal wording entirely and say "one two six five five."

items.explanation: The first statement uses the separate digits with the word point at the decimal.

items.question_text: When may a controller restate a number in a different form?

choices.text: After first stating it the standard way, if extra clarity is needed.
Any time the controller prefers group form over the standard wording.
Only when the pilot specifically asks for the number to be repeated twice.
Before giving the standard form, if the alternate form sounds shorter.

items.explanation: Number clarification comes after the standard 2-4-17 wording and only when needed for clarity.

items.situation: A pilot seems uncertain about frequency 126.55 and you want to clarify both ways of saying it. Build the clarification.
items.target_phrase: ONE TWO SIX POINT FIVE FIVE ONE TWENTY SIX POINT FIFTY FIVE

items.situation: You assign 17,000 feet as "one seven thousand." The pilot sounds unsure and asks for confirmation.
items.para_context: If clarity requires it, a number already stated under 2-4-17 may be restated in either group form or single-digit form.

choices.text: Restate it for clarity, such as "one seven thousand, seventeen thousand."
Repeat only "seventeen" because the word thousand is implied.
Switch permanently to group form and stop using the standard 2-4-17 wording at all.
Refuse to restate it because the standard form may never be followed by a different form.

items.explanation: A clarification restatement is allowed after the standard form when it helps resolve confusion.

items.question_text: How should a flight service station be identified?

choices.text: State the station name followed by the word "radio."
State the station name followed by the word "center."
State the station name followed by the word "tower."
State only the city name if the frequency is dedicated to that station.

items.explanation: Flight service stations are identified with the station name followed by the word radio.

items.situation: You are identifying the Navy Jacksonville tower position. Build the facility identification.
A military approach control facility and a nearby civil approach control facility have similar names. A controller wants to identify the military facility simply by city name and the word approach.
items.para_context: When military and civil facilities in the same general area have similar names, include the military service followed by the name of the military facility and the word approach or tower as appropriate.

choices.text: Include the military service in the identification, such as "Navy Jacksonville Approach."
Use only the city name because the frequency already implies which approach control is being called.
Use the military service name alone without the facility name or function.
Drop the function name and identify only by runway in use.

items.explanation: The military service is added to avoid confusion when similar civil and military facility names exist in the same area.

items.question_text: How should you reply to aircraft with similar sounding identifications?

choices.text: Use the full identification.
Use the same abbreviation both pilots used on initial callup.
Use only the last two digits or letters to keep transmissions shorter.
Use the aircraft type alone until one leaves the frequency.

items.explanation: Similar-sounding aircraft require full identification in replies.

items.question_text: Which prefix must be stated when establishing initial communications with a U.S. registered civil aircraft?

choices.text: November.
Tango.
Medevac.
Executive.

items.explanation: Initial communications with a U.S. registered civil aircraft use the prefix November.

items.situation: Two aircraft on frequency have similar sounding call signs. A controller wants to keep using abbreviated replies because both pilots already checked in and seem to understand each call.
items.para_context: Use the full identification in reply to aircraft with similar sounding identifications. For other aircraft, the same identification the pilot used may be used in reply, but the correct identification must be used after communications have been established.

choices.text: Use the full identification in replies to the similar-sounding aircraft.
Keep the abbreviations because once communications are established abbreviation takes priority over clarity.
Drop both call signs and use aircraft type only until one aircraft changes frequency.
Use the pilot's first name or company name instead of the filed identification.

items.explanation: Clarity controls the reply when aircraft identifications sound alike.

items.question_text: When describing a general aviation or air taxi aircraft in a traffic call, what may be added when it helps identification?

choices.text: The manufacturer's name or color.
The pilot's name or destination.
The full registration number every time.
The aircraft owner's company slogan.

items.explanation: For general aviation and air taxi traffic information, the manufacturer's name or color may be added when advantageous.

items.situation: You are issuing traffic information and need to describe the traffic as a blue and white King Air. Build the description.

You are issuing traffic information to an aircraft following a Boeing 747. Another controller suggests describing the traffic simply as "Boeing Seven Forty-Seven" because the wake category is obvious.
items.para_context: When issuing traffic information to aircraft following a super or heavy aircraft, specify the word super or heavy before the manufacturer's name and model.

choices.text: Include the wake category, such as "Heavy Boeing Seven Forty-Seven."
Use only the model because adding heavy or super is optional in traffic information.
Use the company name only, because the aircraft type is not needed when wake turbulence is possible.
State the registration number instead of the aircraft type.

items.explanation: Traffic information for aircraft following a super or heavy aircraft includes the word super or heavy before the manufacturer's name and model.

items.question_text: How should airspace classes be pronounced in pilot-controller communications?

choices.text: Using ICAO phonetics.
Using plain-English letter names.
Using local slang if both pilots and controllers are domestic.
Using whatever pronunciation the pilot uses first.

items.explanation: Airspace classes are pronounced with ICAO phonetics for clarity.

items.situation: Cessna 123MR is being cleared into Class Bravo airspace. Build the clearance.
items.target_phrase: CESSNA ONE TWO THREE MIKE ROMEO CLEARED TO ENTER BRAVO AIRSPACE

items.question_text: What term may be dropped when referring to airspace in pilot-controller communications?

choices.text: Class.
Airspace.
Cleared.
Enter.

items.explanation: The term class may be dropped when referring to airspace in pilot-controller communications.

items.question_text: How should flight level 275 be spoken?
items.explanation: Flight levels are spoken with the words flight level followed by the separate digits.

choices.text: Flight level two seven five.
Flight level two seventy-five.
Two seven five flight level.
Flight level twenty-seven five.

items.question_text: Complete the prescribed altimeter-setting example: "_____ THREE ZERO ZERO ONE."
items.explanation: Altimeter settings use the word altimeter followed by the separate digits.

choices.text: ALTIMETER

items.question_text: How should frequency 126.55 MHz be stated the first time?
items.explanation: Frequencies are stated with separate digits and the word point where the decimal occurs.

choices.text: One two six point five five.
One twenty-six point fifty-five.
One two six decimal five five.
One twenty-six fifty-five.

items.question_text: For headings, use heading 360 to indicate a north heading.
items.explanation: The paragraph specifically says to use heading 360 degrees to indicate north.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When may a controller restate a number in a different form?
items.explanation: The restatement comes after the standard 2-4-17 wording when extra clarity is needed.

choices.text: After first stating it the standard way, if extra clarity is needed.
Before the standard wording, if group form sounds shorter.
Only after the pilot asks for the number three times.
Only when the number contains a decimal point.

items.question_text: Which two forms may be used when restating a number for clarity?
items.explanation: A clarification restatement may use either group form or single-digit form.

choices.text: Group form or single-digit form.
Roman numeral form or group form.
Any local form or pilot-preferred form.
Alphabetic form or phonetic form.

items.question_text: A controller may skip the standard 2-4-17 wording and jump straight to a group-form restatement if it seems faster.
items.explanation: The alternate form is a clarification restatement after the standard form, not a replacement for it.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: How should a flight service station be identified?
items.explanation: Flight service stations use the station name followed by the word radio.

choices.text: State the station name followed by the word radio.
State the station name followed by the word center.
State the station name followed by the word tower.
State only the city name.

items.question_text: Complete the prescribed facility identification: "COLUMBUS _____."
items.explanation: Airport traffic control towers are identified with the facility name followed by the word tower.

choices.text: TOWER

items.question_text: How should a military approach control facility be identified when it shares the same general area and a similar name with a civil facility?
items.explanation: Include the military service, the facility name, and the word approach.

choices.text: State the military service, the facility name, and the word approach.
State only the city name and the word approach.
State the military service alone.
State the facility name alone without the function name.

items.question_text: When calling or replying on an interphone line that connects only two non-VSCS equipped facilities, the facility name may be omitted.
items.explanation: The paragraph allows omitting the facility name on that specific kind of interphone line.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When replying to aircraft with similar sounding identifications, what should be used?
items.explanation: Use the full identification in reply to aircraft with similar sounding identifications.

choices.text: The full identification.
Any convenient abbreviation used by the controller.
Only the last two digits or letters of the call sign.
The aircraft type without the call sign.

items.question_text: Which prefix is used when establishing initial communications with a U.S. registered civil aircraft?
items.explanation: Initial communications with a U.S. registered civil aircraft use the prefix November.

choices.text: November.
Tango.
Medevac.
Executive.

items.question_text: Foreign civil aircraft registrations should not be abbreviated.
items.explanation: Foreign civil aircraft registrations are not abbreviated.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: On initial contact with a U.S. registered civil aircraft, state the prefix "_____."
items.explanation: The required prefix is November.

choices.text: NOVEMBER

items.question_text: How is an aircraft with an ICAO 3LD and other FAA-authorized call sign identified?
items.explanation: State the call sign followed by the flight number in group form.

choices.text: State the call sign followed by the flight number in group form.
State the call sign followed by the full registration number.
State the aircraft type followed by the last two digits only.
State only the telephony without the flight number.

items.question_text: What prefix is used on initial contact for an air taxi or commercial operator that does not have an FAA-authorized call sign, if that prefix is used by the pilot?
items.explanation: Use the prefix Tango on initial contact if the pilot uses it.

choices.text: Tango.
Medevac.
Executive.
Reach.

items.question_text: When describing a general aviation or air taxi aircraft in a traffic call, what may be added when it helps identification?
items.explanation: The manufacturer's name or color may be added when advantageous.

choices.text: The manufacturer's name or color.
The pilot's name or route of flight.
The complete registration number in every case.
The aircraft owner's company name only.

items.question_text: Complete the approved air-carrier description: "BOEING _____ FIFTY-SEVEN."
items.explanation: The approved traffic description is Boeing Seven Fifty-Seven.

choices.text: SEVEN

items.question_text: When issuing traffic information about an aircraft following a heavy aircraft, what word should come before the manufacturer's name and model?
items.explanation: Use the word heavy before the manufacturer's name and model.

choices.text: Heavy.
Large.
Jet.
Wake.

items.question_text: When issuing traffic information about an aircraft following a super aircraft, what word should come before the manufacturer's name and model?
items.explanation: Use the word super before the manufacturer's name and model.

choices.text: Super.
Heavy.
Large.
Special.

items.question_text: When a military designator is used in traffic information, the numbers are spoken in group form.
items.explanation: Military traffic descriptions may use the military designator with the numbers spoken in group form.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: How should airspace classes be pronounced in pilot-controller communications?
items.explanation: Use ICAO phonetics for the airspace-class letters.

choices.text: Using ICAO phonetics.
Using plain-English letter names.
Using local pronunciation if the pilot sounds domestic.
Using the pilot's own pronunciation, whatever it is.

items.question_text: The word class may be dropped when referring to airspace in pilot-controller communications.
items.explanation: The term class may be dropped in pilot-controller communications.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which transmission uses the approved airspace-class wording?
items.explanation: The approved wording uses ICAO phonetics, such as Bravo airspace.

choices.text: Cessna One Two Three Mike Romeo cleared to enter Bravo airspace.
Cessna One Two Three Mike Romeo cleared to enter B airspace.
Cessna One Two Three Mike Romeo cleared to enter Bee airspace.
Cessna One Two Three Mike Romeo cleared to enter Class Bee airspace.
